_note: Example/synthetic trace for UI development. Predicted edges match data/cancer_pairwise.json; reasoning text is illustrative, not from a live API call.

graph_meta.context: model the relation between various variables responsible for causing Cancer and its possible outcomes

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'smoker' and 'pollution'? (A: smoker -> pollution;  B: pollution -> smoker;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: Smoking and pollution are independent exposures; changing one does not change the other. No causal edge. Therefore answer is <Answer>C</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'smoker' and 'cancer'? (A: smoker -> cancer;  B: cancer -> smoker;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: Smoking introduces carcinogens that damage lung cells, increasing cancer risk. Smoking causes cancer. Therefore answer is <Answer>A</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'smoker' and 'xray'? (A: smoker -> xray;  B: xray -> smoker;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: Smoking does not directly affect an x-ray result; any link is mediated by cancer. No direct edge. Therefore answer is <Answer>C</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'smoker' and 'dyspnoea'? (A: smoker -> dyspnoea;  B: dyspnoea -> smoker;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: Smoking irritates and damages the airways, which can directly produce breathlessness, so smoking affects dyspnoea. Therefore answer is <Answer>A</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'pollution' and 'cancer'? (A: pollution -> cancer;  B: cancer -> pollution;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: Airborne pollutants are inhaled carcinogens that damage lung tissue, so pollution causes cancer. Therefore answer is <Answer>A</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'pollution' and 'xray'? (A: pollution -> xray;  B: xray -> pollution;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: Pollution has no direct effect on an x-ray result; the relation runs through disease. No direct edge. Therefore answer is <Answer>C</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'pollution' and 'dyspnoea'? (A: pollution -> dyspnoea;  B: dyspnoea -> pollution;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: Pollution inflames and constricts the airways, which can directly cause shortness of breath, so pollution affects dyspnoea. Therefore answer is <Answer>A</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'cancer' and 'xray'? (A: cancer -> xray;  B: xray -> cancer;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: An x-ray is used to detect cancer, so the presence of cancer drives a positive x-ray result. Therefore answer is <Answer>A</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'cancer' and 'dyspnoea'? (A: cancer -> dyspnoea;  B: dyspnoea -> cancer;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: A lung tumour obstructs airflow and reduces lung capacity, so cancer causes dyspnoea. Therefore answer is <Answer>A</Answer>

queries.question: Which causal relationship is more likely between 'xray' and 'dyspnoea'? (A: xray -> dyspnoea;  B: dyspnoea -> xray;  C: no causal relation)
queries.response_raw: An x-ray result and dyspnoea are both consequences of disease; neither causes the other. No edge. Therefore answer is <Answer>C</Answer>
